QualiRepression: I have been an observer of XR action for some time as my daughter was heavily involved. I have kept a distance as i've felt concerned about the founders attitude to its members willingness to be arrested, its rhetoric similar to those of cult religions, and its actions affecting regular people and being damaging to the environment. I don't support action that leaves a place in worse condition than it was found. I feel i have no outlet for action that is line with my principals. I joined my daughter for the parliament square action in 2019 to understand what she was involved in. I met many lovely people, saw much generosity, kindness and care. I also witnessed young people being arrested and meeting them afterwards visibly traumatised. I saw police carrying out duties they may or may not believe in, demonised and blamed but i was overwhelmingly concerned by seeing young people whipped up into an emotional response that left them vulnerable. I dont believe we should encourage our young people to be arrested. I dont want to see damage, or hear rhetoric yhat pits communities against each other.  I feel the climate argument has been watered down by political issues such as palestine. I know how they are connected but feel we lose the message when we dilute the subject. My daughter eventually burnt out and also feels that there is no group action that feels like its hitting the mark. Sad as im surexwere not the only ones and hope for an outlet to express our deep concern about climate

OtherGroups_9_TEXT: Tried to fight to save trees from being removed for a development near me and also went to a couple of Transition / Sustainable Northwich Meetings locally and a litter pick
QualiRepression: I KNOW it happens and I am pretty sure I am monitored and not sure if someone is tampering with my car - Read - The Truth About Wuhan - the shit they pulled on that Whistleblower guy
QualiSocMovements:
Sometimes I just feel overwhelmed by the powerlessness against government and law agencies 
It is hard to figure out how I fit in and what I want to do 
I spent 3 years trying to save the trees and its now all documented on the portal - I did a shit tonne of research to see what would and wouldn't be considered, what information had been deliberately withheld and I called it all out - they never saw me coming - pages and pages and pages 
But I wonder if I'll be found dead in a ditch one day 
And it wasn't the local council at fault - they fought to some extent against it - it was the local parish council 
What's the difference between a Democracy and a Dictatorship 
Answer - Our Parish Council 
And what happens on that level happens at every level 
It opened my eyes and a few others ... I say a few .... I think there has been a ripple effect and that's the point 
You think you are doing only something small but it has repercussions on everyone that touches it 
If you do nothing, nothing changes - and I couldn't do nothing
Feelings_Performance:
Honestly - Whatever it takes 
It took me a while to realise the masks I was seeing was Guy Fawkes 
And I'd made the joke about bringing him back

QualiSocMovements: I respect the choices and efforts of XR, I do not participate as I do not believe that direct action will solve this. At worst it risks alienating the issue from mainstream discussion and having a counter productive effect where peoples resentment against protesters replaces their anger at climate change and mass extinction. My own view is in accord with that expressed by Mayer Hillman in around 2018 which was to say it's too late to stop this, the challenge is in adaptation to a low carbon usage lifestyle. This may not be XR's view but I believe he is or was their patron. As for Just Stop Oil and Insulate Britain I have no time for them, no respect for them and think they are a liability to the cause. I hope thats clear. XR Im sure has been an enabling way for people to connect from different countries and I'm sure has a value in coordinating and amplifying the voices of many people. I do not think protests are sufficient to influence politics and it is at this level we need change. I am sympathetic and supportive of the aims of XR but I do not wish to engage with its protests. I am extremely wary of anyone who thinks blocking the M25 is an effective and persuasive means to influence people. I think it just really pisses them off, but each to their own I guess.
Feelings_Performance: I have seen reports, they come across as creative and theatrical but I am not convinced they do anything except get in peoples way. The first die-in was well reported and people seemed to understand the message of it, I think thats become lost now. I think the most powerful thing we can all do is change our own lifestyles to demonstrate our commitment to others. I dont think dressing up necessarily helps.

QualiSocMovements:
I think the climate movement is wasting its time. You cannot change the mindset of those in the establishment. Neither can you change the mindset of those who have been 'educated' by this culture, and 'need' certainty. As you can see by the rise of the 'right wing' mentality, the negative forces acting against the 'climate activists' is existential for them. They will never concede. Being 'right' (correct) is the major part of their position. Conceding that they are wrong is not possible. The psychological underpinnings of their position is totally unconscious. These people don't have any insight, and therefore cannot be reasoned with. Their mindset is set in concrete and cannot be broken.
If you want to understand the human psyche, read Janov (Arthur), and then you might begin to understand the enormity of the task of changing humanity from its exploitation mindset to one of intelligent, ethical stewardship of planet earth. You are wasting your time. The likely fate of humanity is extinction. Crocodiles on the south pole. I hope I'm wrong.
Feelings_Performance: Positive. I like the idea of a consistent image, prodding away at the vestigial conscience of mankind.

QualiSocMovements: I think protest laws are being increased in an undemocratic manner and it greatly concerns me, especially the arrest of Palestine action supporters. This has made me not just distrust the police (I always have) but actively feel the need to avoid and fear them at protests - not only are they not on our side (never were) but are now actively against us. QualiRepression: My first experience of arrest was with XR in October 2019. Then came lockdown and my involvement was in my local area. I was later drawn to JSO because of the focussed nature of the campaign. I appreciated the in depth training and the building of a tightly knit supportive community. My first arrest with XR was a positive experience. I  was prepared for what happened, police station support was there to meet me. My experience in custody was fine. It was even better with JSO as we went into action in teams. As an elderly white middle class woman I have always been treated  well during the arrest process and in custody. I  felt a deep sense of loss when the JSO campaign ended but am still involved as I'm going through the crown court process so am in contact with my trial group - a trial set to happen 3 years after the alleged offence. The possibility of jail time limits how far I'm prepared to take action

QualiSocMovements: Yes. I sign petitions and write to MPs. I do worry that it makes me a target, but I have to put my name to what I truly believe. I am disabled so do not attend events. I worry that my benefits will be docked. I believe I am a thorn in my MPs side, but feel I am being fobbed off and ignored. I will not give up even though I feel my MP is dodging the issues and towing the party line. I will continue to speak openly to anyone who will listen. Word of mouth is the oldest form of getting the word out. I will never stop learning how to help nature. I will never stop sharing my knowledge and proving it's worth with practical proof that we can all rebuild nature by starting at home. Experiment and spread the word. I try to lead by example. I’ve learned so much already. There's no way I can go back now. Too many generations blame the ones before. It's time to grow up and take responsibility here and now. I just wish the general public could plant fruit trees and shrubs in communal areas to preempt the inevitable food security crisis that we are likely to experience before the end of this decade. We need to plant fast. I would follow a movement to physically rebuild our neighbourhoods without the lengthy bureaucracy that is our death warrant. I am currently working on growing trees from seed and self sets. We just need somewhere to plant.
Feelings_Performance: Art is expression. The demonstrations were beautiful and stylish. How could they be ignored. Very disappointed that the BBC do not embrace the sophistication and intellectual vibrancy of these events. Art comes from the heart. An important message such as this needs to be conveyed to the whole world. Creative expression is our best weapon.

You won’t see any corporate oil companies marching the streets, they do what ever if needed to drill oil and make money (not that I agree with their methods or the framework that in which it is done, but they made that framework). I genuinely feel there will be enough talented intelligent dedicated members of the XR group that we could start our own alternative green energy solution. Or reinvent transport options, food distribution, water supply. Anything is possible if you collectivise, yet protests aren’t really action in my opinion, it’s raising the alarms bells sure, it’s holding elected politicians accountable sure, it’s asking for institutional change sure, but it’s not personal action it’s action at a distance, it’s but the requests for action on someone else. QualiSocMovements: I am in my seventies and have been interested or involved in politics for six decades. In all that time I have yet to meet a single person whose views on any issue have been positively influenced by becoming aware of a street demonstration. This country only accounts for under 1% of global emissions and if we were able to decarbonise completely wd have minimal influence on the ultimate outcome. One problem ... and I have had this argument with Greens many times ... is that it is not what is produced that is the problem, but what is consumed. Production will always follow demand. If, setting aside the effects of the present war, we shut down the entire North Sea this afternoon, by Monday that production wd have migrated elsewhere and such a gesture wd not make a tiny iota of difference to global consumption. Indeed, by pushing energy and industrial production offshore we are increasing consumption whilst dumping our problem on others. That is why I feel pretty hopeless.

OtherGroups_9_TEXT: local dark skies group, local green group, local creek cleanup CIC
QualiRepression: Information gathering is just a small part of the establishment's reaction to mass opposition movements using direct action. If you look at history it should come as no surprise! I am intending to sail to the USA sometime in the next decade, so a potential criminal record is a deterrent. However, being monitored doing, whilst standing up for something I believe in, is something I am OK with. It is part of what I believe in, so I am proud to be monitored!
QualiSocMovements: Our duty as citizens, as I see it, is to actively participate in our democracy - so there is a sliding scale from simply turning up to vote, at one end, to actively and intentionally breaking the law with the justification of bigger considerations, at the other. I try and stay within the law but this is increasingly difficult! I guess I am still guided by what has been the law through most of my adult life - it is hard to adjust when the goalposts have been moved!
Feelings_Performance: When I first encountered the Red Rebels I was extremely moved. Their slow, often mournful, synchronised movements were a kind of lament - in many ways communicating our complicated feelings much better than a thousand placards or pages of polemic. But it fascinated me how quickly my brain assimilated them into my 'known knowns', and their novelty and power was dimmed and diminished by familiarity. In the same ways late stage capitalism (as if it is going to end....) seems to have assimilated the climate protest movement. It almost mirrors our ability to see it as just another backdrop against which we carry on as before. This shouldn't be a drive towards a search for ever more novel approaches - the new will arise, but sometimes we need to sit quietly to recognise what it looks like. I think they sparked my re-appraisal of my own approach - which has been to look again at what is around me; to go hyper-local in impacts; to pick up the threads of local activism and take time over difficult conversations with those that aren't yet on board with climate changes that we all need to make.

QualiSocMovements: I have always struggled to have the strength of spirit and confidence to take action that is 'against' something. I fear arrest, violence, damage to my reputation, upsetting my family etc. Most of all, it is instantly depressing for me to be in "opposition", acting "against" something. My mental health just isn't cut out for that kind of action and I have ENDLESS respect for every single person who manages to do it. <3 Whilst accepting, that is not me. With the extreme changes in the law in the UK the last few years, my fears have only got worse. As always, I keep looking for ways to take action that are "for" and PRO and positive, as that is where I shine, where I am confident, able, motivated and strong. I am a musician and a lifelong climate activist in my own, artistic, positively-focused, creative ways. Plus, I am always modelling personal action in my life through decades of my own vehemently eco lifestyle. Thankyou to the people processing this questionnaire. I love and respect you beyond measure + I#m cheering SO hard for you from where I am <3 Peace!

QualiRepression: No, because I don't care.  XR only exists because the government are fascist and under the influence of rich and corporate interests.  XR would not exist under an egalitarian government who work for the interests of ordinary people.  My only worry is, that, I don't believe that ordinary people can win, I believe that the bad guys already won a long time ago.
QualiSocMovements: It means that the government are fascist.  Under an egalitarian government (Scotland is a bit more left than Westminster), the government would actively listen to the concerns of activist groups, and actually do something.  But what do the Westminster central government do?  Arrests and crack down on dissenting voices.  It is because solidarity is intersectional, not only environmental issues, but all of the other issues too.  If one group raises their voice, it may encourage another group of ordinary class to raise theirs too.  Fascist governments have always done this: divide and segregate ordinary class into separate groups (LGBTQ, black, white, English, Scottish...), and give each group different sets of rights, and then watch what happens.  Under an egalitarian government, those rights, those equal rights, would be inalienable and inseparable and indivisible from the human.
Feelings_Performance: I live in the West of Scotland, taobh Siar na h-Alba, and nothing ever happens here.  That is because, where I live, our government, our council, our communities, our neighbours, actually look out for eachother, so we don't need big solutions, because we don't have big problems.  Even further East, in Glas Chu, corporate interests and the rich have not dominated the ordinary class, because we don't let them.  So, broadly speaking, I have not had reason to encounter any of the people pictured.  But I support them.  If I were being oppressed by a fascist government, who are overtly operating in the interests of the rich and corporate class, and working against ordinary citizens, I'd fight much harder.  William Wallace led an army against forces of oppression.
Class: I don't, but if I must, upper-middle.

QualiSocMovements: Absolutely. As a disabled activist I feel doubly vulnerable. I am extremely concerned about the rise of the far right and the impact  I feel this is already having on climate campaigning and other campaigns for social justice. It put me off attending a recent march because I was worried about it being hijacked by counter protesters. I am also concerned by the interests of big tech (particularly meta) and its recent “consent to us using all your data for advertising or pay to use our services” “privacy policy”, profiling and the impact this may or may not have on online protest. I am extremely selective about the platforms I use now as I do not want my data used against me in the future, and I think this (unfortunately) could be a realistic prospect for climate protestors/activists.
